title: The Initialization Protocol
setup: It is your first week as an intern (or trainee). You are invited to a cross-functional kickoff meeting to observe. The Project Manager unexpectedly asks everyone to go around the table for a brief introduction.
trigger: You are the lowest-ranking node in the room. You feel an intense pressure to justify why you were selected for this competitive program. What do you say?

choices.label: The Academic Flex
choices.text: Hi, I’m [Name]. I just graduated from State University. I was president of my club, my capstone project won an award, and I’m really excited to prove myself here.
choices.feedback: You dump irrelevant campus accolades into the network. The senior team mentally categorizes you as a student who still relies on academic validation, not a professional peer.
choices.skill: Confidence is quiet. Past academic trophies have zero value in a current operational environment.

choices.label: The Wallflower
choices.text: Hi, I’m [Name]. I’m just an intern here to learn from everyone and hopefully not get in the way.
choices.feedback: You register as a passive, high-maintenance node. By framing yourself as "just" a learner, you assign the rest of the room the burden of managing you.
choices.skill: Self-deprecation lowers the system's trust in your baseline utility.

choices.label: The Functional Node
choices.text: Hi, I’m [Name]. I recently joined as an intern. For this project, I’ll be supporting the team by handling the initial data sorting. Looking forward to working with you.
choices.feedback: Low latency, highly relevant data. You establish your identity and state your exact utility, bypassing ego entirely.
choices.skill: Signal Calibration: Providing only the specific data the current environment needs to function.

title: The Signal-to-Noise Protocol
setup: A senior team member asks you to review a spreadsheet they compiled before they present it to the department head. You spot a glaring formula error that invalidates their conclusion.
trigger: You need to execute the correction. How do you handle this?

choices.label: The 'A+' Student
choices.text: Post in the main team Slack: "@Senior I found a major calculation error in column D. The correct number is X."
choices.feedback: You optimized for being 'right' but publicly burned a senior node. They will never ask you for help or mentor you again.
choices.skill: Knowing the difference between being technically correct and strategically effective.

choices.label: The Patch
choices.text: Silently fix the formula and save the document without telling them.
choices.feedback: The immediate error is gone, but the root cause remains. They will make the same mistake next time, and you assumed unauthorized risk.
choices.skill: Recognizing that masking system errors prevents system growth.

choices.label: The Alliance
choices.text: Message them privately: "Hey, looking at the sheet, I'm getting a different output in column D. Can we do a quick sanity check on the formula together?"
choices.feedback: You provide a controlled exit vector. They fix the error, save face, and view you as a reliable safety net.
choices.skill: Ego-Preservation: De-escalating errors by allowing others to self-correct.

title: The Attention Radii Protocol
setup: You are at your desk in an open-plan office. Your phone rings, it is your landlord, and you desperately need to take this call to secure your lease.
trigger: The team around you is deep in focus, writing code and drafting reports. How do you handle the call?

choices.label: The Dorm Standard
choices.text: Answer at your desk and speak at a normal volume, arguing about the lease terms.
choices.feedback: You broadcast personal chaos into a shared professional space, breaking the focus of five people around you.
choices.skill: Respecting the acoustic boundaries of a shared environment.

choices.label: The Martyr
choices.text: Decline the call to avoid being a nuisance, risking your housing situation.
choices.feedback: Your personal infrastructure degrades, which will inevitably bleed into your professional performance later.
choices.skill: Understanding that self-sacrifice is an unsustainable operating model.

choices.label: Spatial Isolation
choices.text: Hit mute, grab your laptop, walk swiftly to the corridor or a phone booth, and return the call.
choices.feedback: You handle your critical personal data without leaking acoustic noise into the team's environment.
choices.skill: Resource Management: Utilizing physical space to compartmentalize conflicting tasks.

title: The Early Warning Protocol
setup: On Monday morning, you are assigned your first solo deliverable, due on Friday at noon. On Wednesday afternoon, you realize a critical software license you need to finish the work hasn't been approved by IT.
trigger: You are officially blocked. If you don't get the software today, missing the Friday deadline is mathematically certain. What do you do?

choices.label: The Heroic Grind
choices.text: Keep quiet. You decide to work late on Thursday using free, clunky workarounds, hoping you can scrape together something passable by the deadline.
choices.feedback: You deliver a subpar product at the last minute. Your manager didn't know you were struggling and couldn't help. You assumed unauthorized risk and failed the team.
choices.skill: Predictability is better than heroism. Hiding your blockers creates systemic risk.

choices.label: The Deadline Excuse
choices.text: Wait until Friday morning when the manager asks for the work, then explain that IT never gave you the license so you couldn't finish it.
choices.feedback: You threw IT under the bus, but the manager blames you. In the professional world, a blocked dependency is your responsibility to escalate, not an excuse for failure.
choices.skill: Owning your dependencies. An early warning is a project update; a late warning is just an excuse.

choices.label: The Proactive Escalation
choices.text: Message your manager immediately: "Heads up, I'm blocked on the IT license. If it doesn't clear by EOD, the Friday deadline is at risk. Should I escalate this to the IT director?"
choices.feedback: You protected the system. Your manager appreciates the heads-up and steps in to expedite the IT request. You proved you can manage risk reliably.
choices.skill: Flagging blockers early. Professionals communicate risk days before it becomes a failure.

title: The Feedback Reception Protocol
setup: You submit your first major presentation deck to your manager. They send it back completely covered in comments, essentially tearing apart the structure and asking for massive rewrites.
trigger: In college, this level of red ink meant you failed. You feel a rush of defensive anxiety. How do you respond?

choices.label: The Defense Attorney
choices.text: Draft a long email explaining your thought process for every single slide, subtly proving why your original way actually made sense.
choices.feedback: You optimized for protecting your ego. The manager doesn't care about your justifications; they just want the deck fixed. You are now seen as defensive and difficult to coach.
choices.skill: Feedback is not a debate. Defensiveness slows down the iteration cycle.

choices.label: The Apologetic Collapse
choices.text: Message them: "I am so sorry, I totally missed the mark on this. I'll just delete it and start over. Let me know if you'd rather just have someone else do it."
choices.feedback: You force your manager into the role of a therapist. Instead of just reviewing your work, they now have to manage your emotional state and reassure you.
choices.skill: Separating self-worth from work output. Apologize for careless mistakes, but never apologize for receiving standard feedback.

choices.label: The Clinical Processor
choices.text: Reply: "Got the notes. Thanks for the thorough review. I see where you want to take the structure. I'll get v2 back to you by tomorrow afternoon."
choices.feedback: You absorb the impact like a professional. You acknowledge the data, set a new timeline, and move forward without emotional friction. You are highly coachable.
choices.skill: Processing feedback clinically. A red pen is a calibration tool, not an attack on your intelligence.
